Lemma — Adding intervention nodes commutes with disjoint node-splitting hard interventions

Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CADMG (def def-cdmg-types, item i., equivalently a CDMG of def def-cdmg that is acyclic in the sense of def def-acylic); throughout we use the notation of def not-cdmg and recall the typing and axioms of def def-cdmg, namely $J \cap V = \emptyset$, $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$, $L \subseteq V \times V / ((v_1, v_2) \sim (v_2, v_1))$ (the unordered-pair quotient, realised in the project's Lean encoding by Mathlib's $\text{Sym}_2(V)$ via the cdmg_typed_edges refactor; swap-symmetry $(v_1, v_2) \sim (v_2, v_1)$ is therefore definitional and no separate symmetry axiom is needed), and $L$ irreflexive ($(v_1, v_2) \in L \Rightarrow v_1 \neq v_2$). We rely throughout on the node-splitting hard-intervention (SWIG) construction $\swig$ of def def:G_node-splitting_intervention and the intervention-node extension $\doit(I_{\cdot})$ of def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes. Let \[ W_1 \subseteq V \quad\text{and}\quad W_2 \subseteq J \cup V \] be two subsets of nodes of $G$ (both automatically finite, since $J \cup V$ is finite by def def-cdmg; this matches the project's Lean encoding of $W_1$ and $W_2$ as Finsets). The typings $W_1 \subseteq V$ (strictly tighter than the ambient $J \cup V$, since $W_1$ must lie on the output side) and $W_2 \subseteq J \cup V$ (overlap with $J$ is permitted) are dictated by the preconditions of the two constructors involved: $\swig$ acts only on output nodes (def def:G_node-splitting_intervention, “Let $W \subseteq V$”), while $\doit(I_{\cdot})$ admits an arbitrary subset of the carrier (def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes, “Let $W \subseteq J \cup V$”). The two subsets are subject to the disjointness side condition \[ W_1 \cap W_2 \;=\; \emptyset. \] All three quantifiers “for every CADMG $G$”, “for every $W_1 \subseteq V$”, and “for every $W_2 \subseteq J \cup V$ with $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$” are read universally; in particular the (vacuously disjoint) corner cases $W_1 = W_2 = \emptyset$, $W_1 = \emptyset$ alone, and $W_2 = \emptyset$ alone are admitted, and so are the following two corner cases governed by the $I_j := j$ convention of def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes:

  • $W_2 \subseteq J$ (so $W_2 \sm J = \emptyset$): the extension $G_{\doit(I_{W_2})} = G$ is the identity by def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes (no fresh intervention nodes and no new edges are added, since the $I_j := j$ shorthand for $j \in J \cap W_2 = W_2$ is purely notational); the displayed equality below then degenerates to $G_{\swig(W_1)} = G_{\swig(W_1)}$, which is trivially true. The $W_2 \subseteq J$ case is admitted by the statement (read literally per def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes, not naively as “$|W_2|$ fresh nodes added”).
  • $W_2 \cap J \neq \emptyset$ and $W_2 \cap V \neq \emptyset$ (the partial case): the extension $G_{\doit(I_{W_2})}$ adds fresh intervention nodes $\lC I_w \st w \in W_2 \sm J \rC$ and edges $\lC (I_w, w) \st w \in W_2 \sm J \rC$ only for the $V$-elements $W_2 \sm J = W_2 \cap V$ of $W_2$, and acts as the identity on the $J$-elements $W_2 \cap J$ (the $I_j := j$ shorthand contributes no new node and no new edge there). Sub-case admitted; the equality below holds with the $W_2 \sm J$ parametrisation visible in the directed-edge component (per def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes, item iii.).

Reading of “CADMG” in the LN's prose. The LN's literal text opens with “Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CADMG” and refers in the prose to “the CADMG that arises from $\ldots$” for both sides of the displayed equality. Both sides are produced by applying $\swig$ (def def:G_node-splitting_intervention) and $\doit(I_{\cdot})$ (def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes) in some order to $G$. The constructor of def def:G_node-splitting_intervention requires the CADMG hypothesis on its input – on $G$ for the LHS's inner $\swig(W_1)$, and on $G_{\doit(I_{W_2})}$ for the RHS's outer $\swig(W_1)$. Acyclicity of $G_{\doit(I_{W_2})}$ under the CADMG hypothesis on $G$ is established by claim_3_13 (AcyclicHardInterventionTopologicalOrder in the SWIG-analog presentation of this section, more precisely the remark right after def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes in the LN, which states that acyclicity of $G$ implies acyclicity of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ for every $W \subseteq J \cup V$). The constructor of def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes only requires its input to be a CDMG (not a CADMG); since both $G$ and $G_{\swig(W_1)}$ are CDMGs (the latter by the CDMG-axiom check at the end of def def:G_node-splitting_intervention), the inner $\doit(I_{W_2})$ on either side is defined regardless of acyclicity. Hence both sides of the displayed equality are CADMGs. At the underlying CDMG-type level, the asserted equality below is first an equality of CDMGs (def def-cdmg) – componentwise on the four components $(J, V, E, L)$ – and the upgrade to an equality of CADMGs is automatic once both sides are known to be acyclic CDMGs (acyclicity is a propositional condition on a fixed CDMG, transported along the CDMG-equality).

Disambiguation of the $\swig(\cdot)$ and $\doit(I_{\cdot})$ notation. The only $\swig$ in this lemma is $\swig(W_1)$ with $W_1 \subseteq V$, and the only $\doit$ in this lemma is $\doit(I_{W_2})$ with the $I$-tagged argument $I_{W_2}$ (not the bare-node-set argument $W_2$). In particular, the hard-intervention reading $G_{\doit(W)}$ of def def:G_hard_intervention (which would consume a bare node set $W$ and produce a CDMG with $J_{\doit(W)} := J \cup W$ etc.) is not invoked here; nor is any SWIG-of-$I$-tagged-set notation. Concretely:

  • $G_{\swig(W_1)}$ denotes the node-splitting hard intervention on $W_1$ (def def:G_node-splitting_intervention, which requires $G$ to be a CADMG – matching the standing hypothesis on $G$, and giving the SWIG construction directly via four set-builders rather than via an intermediate node-split). Its components are $J_{\swig(W_1)} := J \dcup W_1^i$, $V_{\swig(W_1)} := (V \sm W_1) \dcup W_1^o$, $E_{\swig(W_1)} := \lC (v_1^i, v_2^o) \st (v_1, v_2) \in E \rC$, and $L_{\swig(W_1)} := \lC (v_1^o, v_2^o) \st (v_1, v_2) \in L \rC$, where $W_1^o := \lC w^o \st w \in W_1 \rC$ and $W_1^i := \lC w^i \st w \in W_1 \rC$ are the two type-level tagged copies of $W_1$ introduced in def def:G_node-splitting_intervention (so $W_1^o \cap W_1^i = \emptyset$ and both $W_1^o$ and $W_1^i$ are disjoint from every element of $J \cup V$, the latter automatically by the tagged construction), and the shorthand $v^o := v^i := v$ is used for $v \in J \cup (V \sm W_1)$ inside the set-builders for $E_{\swig(W_1)}$ and $L_{\swig(W_1)}$ (def def:G_node-splitting_intervention, items i.–iv.). The input-side tagged copy $W_1^i$ sits in $J_{\swig(W_1)}$ and the output-side tagged copy $W_1^o$ sits in $V_{\swig(W_1)}$.
  • $G_{\doit(I_{W_2})}$ denotes the extended CDMG obtained by adding intervention nodes for $W_2$ (def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes). Its components are $J_{\doit(I_{W_2})} := J \cup \lC I_w \st w \in W_2 \sm J \rC$, $V_{\doit(I_{W_2})} := V$, $E_{\doit(I_{W_2})} := E \cup \lC (I_w, w) \st w \in W_2 \sm J \rC$, and $L_{\doit(I_{W_2})} := L$, where $\lC I_w \st w \in W_2 \sm J \rC$ is the family of fresh intervention symbols introduced by def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes (type-disjoint from $J \cup V$ and pairwise type-disjoint), and the notational shorthand $I_j := j$ for $j \in J \cap W_2$ means no new node is introduced and no new edge is added on the $J \cap W_2$ branch. The output-node set $V$ and the bidirected-edge set $L$ are untouched.

Well-typedness of the iterated operations on both sides. Each iterated operation appearing in the displayed equality below is well-typed per its constructor's precondition:

  • LHS, $\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(I_{W_2})}$. The inner $G_{\swig(W_1)}$ is defined per def def:G_node-splitting_intervention from the standing $W_1 \subseteq V$ and the CADMG hypothesis on $G$. By items i.\ and ii.\ of def def:G_node-splitting_intervention, the carrier of $G_{\swig(W_1)}$ is \[ J_{\swig(W_1)} \cup V_{\swig(W_1)} \;=\; \lp J \dcup W_1^i \rp \;\cup\; \lp (V \sm W_1) \dcup W_1^o \rp, \] with the canonical inclusion (the “unsplit” map) $\iota_{\swig} \colon J \cup (V \sm W_1) \hookrightarrow J_{\swig(W_1)} \cup V_{\swig(W_1)}$ acting as $v \mapsto v$ – and the elements of $W_1$ themselves are not in the image of $\iota_{\swig}$, having been split into the tagged copies $W_1^o, W_1^i$. The disjointness $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$ together with $W_2 \subseteq J \cup V$ gives $W_2 \subseteq J \cup (V \sm W_1)$, so $\iota_{\swig}$ restricts to an inclusion $W_2 \hookrightarrow J_{\swig(W_1)} \cup V_{\swig(W_1)}$ identifying $W_2$ with its image $\iota_{\swig}[W_2]$ as the unsplit-untagged copy of $W_2$ inside the SWIG carrier. Hence the outer $\doit(I_{W_2})$ is defined per def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes applied to $G_{\swig(W_1)}$ with the subset $\iota_{\swig}[W_2] \subseteq J_{\swig(W_1)} \cup V_{\swig(W_1)}$ (def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes only requires its input graph to be a CDMG, and $G_{\swig(W_1)}$ is a CDMG by the axiom-check at the end of def def:G_node-splitting_intervention). Under this identification, $\iota_{\swig}[W_2] \cap J_{\swig(W_1)} = W_2 \cap J$ (the $J$-elements of $W_2$ remain in $J$, with no node addition under the $I_j := j$ convention) and $\iota_{\swig}[W_2] \sm J_{\swig(W_1)} = W_2 \sm J \subseteq V \sm W_1$ (the $V$-elements of $W_2$ sit on the untagged $V \sm W_1$ piece, with one fresh $I_w$ added for each).
  • RHS, $\lp G_{\doit(I_{W_2})} \rp_{\swig(W_1)}$. The inner $G_{\doit(I_{W_2})}$ is defined per def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes from the standing $W_2 \subseteq J \cup V$ on the CDMG underlying the CADMG $G$. By items i.\ and ii.\ of def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes, the carrier of $G_{\doit(I_{W_2})}$ is \[ J_{\doit(I_{W_2})} \cup V_{\doit(I_{W_2})} \;=\; \lp J \cup \lC I_w \st w \in W_2 \sm J \rC \rp \;\cup\; V, \] with the canonical inclusion (the “unsplit” map) $\iota_{\doit} \colon J \cup V \hookrightarrow J_{\doit(I_{W_2})} \cup V_{\doit(I_{W_2})}$ acting as $v \mapsto v$ for all $v \in J \cup V$ (every original node of $G$ embeds untagged into the extended carrier; only the fresh symbols $I_w$ for $w \in W_2 \sm J$ are new). Since $W_1 \subseteq V = V_{\doit(I_{W_2})}$, the inclusion $\iota_{\doit}$ restricts to an inclusion $W_1 \hookrightarrow V_{\doit(I_{W_2})}$ identifying $W_1$ with its image $\iota_{\doit}[W_1]$ on the output side. Hence the outer $\swig(W_1)$ is defined per def def:G_node-splitting_intervention applied to $G_{\doit(I_{W_2})}$ with the subset $\iota_{\doit}[W_1] \subseteq V_{\doit(I_{W_2})}$. The CADMG-hypothesis precondition of def def:G_node-splitting_intervention on $G_{\doit(I_{W_2})}$ is met because $G_{\doit(I_{W_2})}$ is acyclic whenever $G$ is, by the LN's remark immediately following def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes (already noted under “Reading of `CADMG' ” above).

Distinct carriers and the canonical relabelling identifying them. The LHS and RHS of the displayed equality live in formally distinct iterated-tagged-sum carriers:

  • the LHS carrier is $J_{\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(I_{W_2})}} \cup V_{\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(I_{W_2})}}$, obtained by first splitting $W_1 \subseteq V$ off into the tagged copies $W_1^o, W_1^i$ (def def:G_node-splitting_intervention) and then adding fresh intervention symbols $\lC I_w \st w \in W_2 \sm J \rC$ on top (def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes); concretely \[ J \cup W_1^i \cup \lC I_w \st w \in W_2 \sm J \rC \;\cup\; (V \sm W_1) \cup W_1^o, \] with all four unions automatically disjoint by the tagged-copy disjointness of $W_1^o, W_1^i$ from $J \cup V$ and from each other, and by the freshness of the intervention symbols $\lC I_w \st w \in W_2 \sm J \rC$ from $J \cup V$ and from $W_1^o, W_1^i$ (the last clause follows from the tagged construction together with $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$, which ensures no $I_w$ symbol is accidentally identified with any $w' \in W_1$);
  • the RHS carrier is $J_{\lp G_{\doit(I_{W_2})} \rp_{\swig(W_1)}} \cup V_{\lp G_{\doit(I_{W_2})} \rp_{\swig(W_1)}}$, obtained by first adding fresh intervention symbols $\lC I_w \st w \in W_2 \sm J \rC$ (def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes) and then splitting $W_1 \subseteq V$ off into the tagged copies $W_1^o, W_1^i$ (def def:G_node-splitting_intervention); concretely \[ J \cup \lC I_w \st w \in W_2 \sm J \rC \cup W_1^i \;\cup\; (V \sm W_1) \cup W_1^o, \] again with all four unions automatically disjoint.

The two carriers are equal as plain sets of symbols (both are the union $J \cup (V \sm W_1) \cup W_1^o \cup W_1^i \cup \lC I_w \st w \in W_2 \sm J \rC$, with the $\cup$ between any two of the five pieces automatically disjoint), and the canonical relabelling identifying them is the obvious one: the unsplit copy of every $v \in J \cup V$ maps to itself on either side; the tagged copies $w^o \in W_1^o$ and $w^i \in W_1^i$ on the LHS (introduced by “first split”) are identified with the tagged copies $w^o \in W_1^o$ and $w^i \in W_1^i$ on the RHS (introduced by “last split”) via the identity on $W_1$; and the fresh intervention symbols $I_w$ for $w \in W_2 \sm J$ on the LHS (introduced by “last extend”) are identified with the fresh intervention symbols $I_w$ for $w \in W_2 \sm J$ on the RHS (introduced by “first extend”) via the identity on $W_2 \sm J$. The asserted equality of CADMGs below is read componentwise on the four components $(J, V, E, L)$ of def def-cdmg up to this canonical relabelling; equivalently, when each carrier is identified with the common set just displayed, the four components of the LHS and the four components of the RHS coincide as subsets of (the carrier, the carrier $\times$ carrier with appropriate input–output typing, and the output-side carrier squared).

The conclusion. With the well-typedness and carrier identifications spelled out above, the following equality of CADMGs holds: \[ \lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(I_{W_2})} \;=\; \lp G_{\doit(I_{W_2})} \rp_{\swig(W_1)}. \] Componentwise – which is the unique unpacking the equality admits, since CDMGs are tuples $(J, V, E, L)$ of def def-cdmg – the equality unfolds into the four field-by-field identifications (under the canonical relabelling of the previous paragraph): \[\begin{aligned} J_{\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(I_{W_2})}} \;&=\; J_{\lp G_{\doit(I_{W_2})} \rp_{\swig(W_1)}} \;=\; J \;\cup\; W_1^i \;\cup\; \lC I_w \st w \in W_2 \sm J \rC, \\ V_{\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(I_{W_2})}} \;&=\; V_{\lp G_{\doit(I_{W_2})} \rp_{\swig(W_1)}} \;=\; (V \sm W_1) \;\cup\; W_1^o, \\ E_{\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(I_{W_2})}} \;&=\; E_{\lp G_{\doit(I_{W_2})} \rp_{\swig(W_1)}} \;=\; \lC (v_1^i, v_2^o) \st (v_1, v_2) \in E \rC \;\cup\; \lC (I_w, w) \st w \in W_2 \sm J \rC, \\ L_{\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(I_{W_2})}} \;&=\; L_{\lp G_{\doit(I_{W_2})} \rp_{\swig(W_1)}} \;=\; \lC (v_1^o, v_2^o) \st (v_1, v_2) \in L \rC, \end{aligned}\] where on the right-hand sides the tagged shorthands $v_1^i$, $v_1^o$, $v_2^o$ for $v_1, v_2 \in J \cup V$ are read per def def:G_node-splitting_intervention, items i.–iv., i.e.\ $v_k^o := v_k^i := v_k$ for $v_k \in J \cup (V \sm W_1)$ (untagged) and $v_k^o \in W_1^o$, $v_k^i \in W_1^i$ are the tagged copies when $v_k \in W_1$; and where in the second set-builder of the $E$-component the symbol $w$ for $w \in W_2 \sm J$ refers to its unsplit-untagged image $\iota_{\swig}[w] = w \in V \sm W_1$ (well-defined since $w \in W_2 \sm J \subseteq V \sm W_1$ by $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$ and $W_2 \sm J \subseteq V$). The conjunctive unpacking of the displayed equality into these four field-by-field equalities is deferred to the proof.

Proof.

We verify the asserted equality componentwise on the four components $(J, V, E, L)$ of def def-cdmg, comparing the LHS $\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(I_{W_2})}$ and the RHS $\lp G_{\doit(I_{W_2})} \rp_{\swig(W_1)}$ field by field after a single SWIG-shorthand setup paragraph. All four well-typednesses needed below (existence of $G_{\swig(W_1)}$, of $G_{\doit(I_{W_2})}$, of the outer $\doit(I_{W_2})$ on $G_{\swig(W_1)}$, and of the outer $\swig(W_1)$ on $G_{\doit(I_{W_2})}$) are licensed by the corresponding bullets of the “Well-typedness” paragraph in the statement block above, which we cite without restating.

Notational setup – the SWIG shorthand on $W_2$. Per def def:G_node-splitting_intervention, items i.–iv., for each $w \in W_1$ the SWIG construction produces two type-disjoint tagged copies $w^o \in W_1^o$ and $w^i \in W_1^i$ (type-disjoint from $J \cup V$ and from each other), and for each $v \in J \cup (V \sm W_1)$ the shorthand \[ v^o \;:=\; v^i \;:=\; v \] records that no copy is made (the unsplit-untagged image $v \mapsto v$). The disjointness hypothesis $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$ combined with $W_2 \subseteq J \cup V$ gives $W_2 \subseteq J \cup (V \sm W_1)$, hence every $w \in W_2$ lies in $J \cup (V \sm W_1)$, so \[ w^o \;=\; w^i \;=\; w \qquad \text{for every } w \in W_2. \] In particular for every $w \in W_2 \sm J \subseteq V \sm W_1$ we have $w^o = w$ (unsplit-untagged on the output side); this identity will be consumed below in the RHS directed-edge calculation when rewriting fresh intervention edges from their SWIG-tagged shape $I_w^i \tuh w^o$ into the bare shape $I_w \tuh w$.

Step 1: computing the RHS, $\lp G_{\doit(I_{W_2})} \rp_{\swig(W_1)}$.

Inner operation $G_{\doit(I_{W_2})}$. Applying def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes to $G$ with the subset $W_2 \subseteq J \cup V$ (well-typedness: def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes only requires its input to be a CDMG, which $G$ is) yields $G_{\doit(I_{W_2})} = (J', V', E', L')$ with \[\begin{aligned} J' &\;:=\; J \;\dcup\; \lC I_w \st w \in W_2 \sm J \rC, & V' &\;:=\; V, \\ E' &\;:=\; E \;\dcup\; \lC (I_w, w) \st w \in W_2 \sm J \rC, & L' &\;:=\; L, \end{aligned}\] where the fresh intervention symbols $\lC I_w \st w \in W_2 \sm J \rC$ are type-disjoint from $J \cup V$ and pairwise type-disjoint per def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes (the disjoint-union symbols $\dcup$ in $J'$ and $E'$ are automatic by this freshness clause). Note that the $W_2 \sm J$ parametrisation – not the naive $W_2$ – is dictated by items i.\ and iii.\ of def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes, and reflects the $I_j := j$ convention: for $j \in J \cap W_2$ no fresh symbol $I_j$ is introduced and no edge $(I_j, j) = (j, j)$ is added.

Outer operation $\swig(W_1)$ on $G_{\doit(I_{W_2})}$. The CADMG-input precondition of def def:G_node-splitting_intervention on $G_{\doit(I_{W_2})}$ is met because $G_{\doit(I_{W_2})}$ is a CDMG (by the axiom-check at the end of def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes) and acyclicity of $G$ transports to acyclicity of $G_{\doit(I_{W_2})}$ by claim_3_13 (the LN's remark immediately following def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes, asserting that acyclicity of $G$ implies acyclicity of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ for every $W \subseteq J \cup V$). The splitting-subset precondition $W_1 \subseteq V'$ is met because $V' = V$ and $W_1 \subseteq V$ by hypothesis.

Applying def def:G_node-splitting_intervention, items i.–iv., to the input $G_{\doit(I_{W_2})} = (J', V', E', L')$ with splitting subset $W_1 \subseteq V'$, and using the SWIG shorthand $v^o := v^i := v$ for $v \in J' \cup (V' \sm W_1)$ (in particular $I_w^o = I_w^i = I_w$ for every fresh symbol $I_w \in \lC I_w \st w \in W_2 \sm J \rC \subseteq J'$, and $w^o = w^i = w$ for every $w \in W_2 \sm J \subseteq V \sm W_1 = V' \sm W_1$ – the latter using $w \notin W_1$ from $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$, which is the disjointness-consuming step): \[\begin{aligned} J_{\lp G_{\doit(I_{W_2})} \rp_{\swig(W_1)}} &\;=\; J' \dcup W_1^i \\ &\;=\; J \;\dcup\; \lC I_w \st w \in W_2 \sm J \rC \;\dcup\; W_1^i, \\[0.5em] V_{\lp G_{\doit(I_{W_2})} \rp_{\swig(W_1)}} &\;=\; (V' \sm W_1) \dcup W_1^o \;=\; (V \sm W_1) \dcup W_1^o, \\[0.5em] E_{\lp G_{\doit(I_{W_2})} \rp_{\swig(W_1)}} &\;=\; \lC v_1^i \tuh v_2^o \st v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E' \rC, \\ L_{\lp G_{\doit(I_{W_2})} \rp_{\swig(W_1)}} &\;=\; \lC v_1^o \huh v_2^o \st v_1 \huh v_2 \in L' \rC. \end{aligned}\] The bidirected-edge component simplifies immediately using $L' = L$: \[ L_{\lp G_{\doit(I_{W_2})} \rp_{\swig(W_1)}} \;=\; \lC v_1^o \huh v_2^o \st v_1 \huh v_2 \in L \rC. \] For the directed-edge component, splitting the source $E' = E \;\dcup\; \lC (I_w, w) \st w \in W_2 \sm J \rC$ across the disjoint union under the set-builder $\lC v_1^i \tuh v_2^o \st (v_1, v_2) \in \cdot \rC$ (which preserves disjoint unions of source sets because the SWIG relabelling $(v_1, v_2) \mapsto (v_1^i, v_2^o)$ is injective on $J \cup V$ – the only non-trivial action is the splitting of $W_1 \subseteq V$ into the tagged copies, which is itself injective by def def:G_node-splitting_intervention), we obtain \[ E_{\lp G_{\doit(I_{W_2})} \rp_{\swig(W_1)}} \;=\; \lC v_1^i \tuh v_2^o \st v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E \rC \;\dcup\; \lC I_w^i \tuh w^o \st w \in W_2 \sm J \rC. \] We rewrite the second set-builder into the bare shape $I_w \tuh w$ by applying the SWIG shorthand twice:

  • $I_w^i = I_w$ because $I_w \in J' \subseteq J' \cup (V' \sm W_1)$ (the fresh symbol $I_w$ sits in the input side of the inner CDMG, hence is on the unsplit-untagged side of the SWIG shorthand);
  • $w^o = w$ because $w \in W_2 \sm J \subseteq V \sm W_1 = V' \sm W_1$ (here is where the disjointness hypothesis $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$ is consumed: it gives $W_2 \sm J \subseteq V \sm W_1$).

Hence $\lC I_w^i \tuh w^o \st w \in W_2 \sm J \rC = \lC I_w \tuh w \st w \in W_2 \sm J \rC$, and the directed-edge component finalises as \[ E_{\lp G_{\doit(I_{W_2})} \rp_{\swig(W_1)}} \;=\; \lC v_1^i \tuh v_2^o \st v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E \rC \;\dcup\; \lC I_w \tuh w \st w \in W_2 \sm J \rC. \]

Collecting the four components: \[\begin{aligned} J_{\lp G_{\doit(I_{W_2})} \rp_{\swig(W_1)}} &\;=\; J \;\dcup\; \lC I_w \st w \in W_2 \sm J \rC \;\dcup\; W_1^i, \\ V_{\lp G_{\doit(I_{W_2})} \rp_{\swig(W_1)}} &\;=\; (V \sm W_1) \;\dcup\; W_1^o, \\ E_{\lp G_{\doit(I_{W_2})} \rp_{\swig(W_1)}} &\;=\; \lC v_1^i \tuh v_2^o \st v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E \rC \;\dcup\; \lC I_w \tuh w \st w \in W_2 \sm J \rC, \\ L_{\lp G_{\doit(I_{W_2})} \rp_{\swig(W_1)}} &\;=\; \lC v_1^o \huh v_2^o \st v_1 \huh v_2 \in L \rC. \end{aligned}\]

Step 2: computing the LHS, $\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(I_{W_2})}$.

Inner operation $G_{\swig(W_1)}$. Applying def def:G_node-splitting_intervention to $G$ with splitting subset $W_1 \subseteq V$ (well-typedness: $G$ is a CADMG and $W_1 \subseteq V$ by hypothesis) yields $G_{\swig(W_1)} = (\hat J, \hat V, \hat E, \hat L)$ with \[\begin{aligned} \hat J &\;:=\; J \dcup W_1^i, & \hat V &\;:=\; (V \sm W_1) \dcup W_1^o, \\ \hat E &\;:=\; \lC v_1^i \tuh v_2^o \st v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E \rC, & \hat L &\;:=\; \lC v_1^o \huh v_2^o \st v_1 \huh v_2 \in L \rC, \end{aligned}\] where the tagged copies $W_1^o, W_1^i$ are type-disjoint from $J \cup V$ and from each other (def def:G_node-splitting_intervention).

A carrier-set identity: $W_2 \sm \hat J = W_2 \sm J$. The outer operation $\doit(I_{W_2})$ refers to the input CDMG $G_{\swig(W_1)}$ through its $J$-component $\hat J$ in the form $W_2 \sm \hat J$ (the index set of the fresh intervention symbols and fresh edges, per items i.\ and iii.\ of def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes). Computing, \[ W_2 \cap \hat J \;=\; W_2 \cap \lp J \dcup W_1^i \rp \;=\; (W_2 \cap J) \;\cup\; (W_2 \cap W_1^i). \] By the type-disjointness clause of def def:G_node-splitting_intervention (the tagged copy $W_1^i$ is type-disjoint from $J \cup V$) and $W_2 \subseteq J \cup V$, the second intersection is empty: $W_2 \cap W_1^i = \emptyset$. Hence $W_2 \cap \hat J = W_2 \cap J$, and therefore \[ W_2 \sm \hat J \;=\; W_2 \sm J. \] (Note that this carrier identity does not consume the disjointness hypothesis $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$ – the type-disjointness of $W_1^i$ from $J \cup V$ suffices on its own. The disjointness hypothesis enters the LHS calculation elsewhere, namely in identifying the head of each fresh edge $I_w \tuh w$ with the unsplit-untagged copy of $w$ inside $\hat V$; see the directed-edge step below.)

Outer operation $\doit(I_{W_2})$ on $G_{\swig(W_1)}$. Def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes requires its input to be a CDMG (which $G_{\swig(W_1)}$ is, by the axiom-check at the end of def def:G_node-splitting_intervention) and its splitting subset to lie in $\hat J \cup \hat V$. The latter is supplied by the unsplit inclusion $\iota_{\swig} \colon J \cup (V \sm W_1) \hookrightarrow \hat J \cup \hat V$ from the statement block, which under $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$ identifies $W_2 \subseteq J \cup (V \sm W_1)$ with its untagged image inside $\hat J \cup \hat V$.

Applying def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes, items i.–iv., to the input $G_{\swig(W_1)} = (\hat J, \hat V, \hat E, \hat L)$ with subset $W_2$, and using the carrier identity $W_2 \sm \hat J = W_2 \sm J$ established above to rewrite the index set: \[\begin{aligned} J_{\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(I_{W_2})}} &\;=\; \hat J \;\dcup\; \lC I_w \st w \in W_2 \sm \hat J \rC \;=\; \hat J \;\dcup\; \lC I_w \st w \in W_2 \sm J \rC \\ &\;=\; J \;\dcup\; W_1^i \;\dcup\; \lC I_w \st w \in W_2 \sm J \rC, \\[0.5em] V_{\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(I_{W_2})}} &\;=\; \hat V \;=\; (V \sm W_1) \dcup W_1^o, \\[0.5em] E_{\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(I_{W_2})}} &\;=\; \hat E \;\dcup\; \lC (I_w, w) \st w \in W_2 \sm \hat J \rC \;=\; \hat E \;\dcup\; \lC (I_w, w) \st w \in W_2 \sm J \rC \\ &\;=\; \lC v_1^i \tuh v_2^o \st v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E \rC \;\dcup\; \lC I_w \tuh w \st w \in W_2 \sm J \rC, \\[0.5em] L_{\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(I_{W_2})}} &\;=\; \hat L \;=\; \lC v_1^o \huh v_2^o \st v_1 \huh v_2 \in L \rC, \end{aligned}\] where in the directed-edge component the head $w$ of each fresh intervention edge $I_w \tuh w$ (for $w \in W_2 \sm J$) is read as the unsplit-untagged image of $w$ inside $\hat V = (V \sm W_1) \dcup W_1^o$. This image is well-defined precisely because $w \in W_2 \sm J \subseteq V \sm W_1 \subseteq \hat V$, where the inclusion $W_2 \sm J \subseteq V \sm W_1$ is the disjointness-consuming step (it uses $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$). The bidirected-edge component is unchanged by $\doit(I_{W_2})$ per item iv.\ of def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes.

Step 3: matching the four components.

Comparing the four components produced by Step 1 (RHS) and Step 2 (LHS) line by line:

  • Input nodes. \[ J_{\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(I_{W_2})}} \;=\; J \;\dcup\; W_1^i \;\dcup\; \lC I_w \st w \in W_2 \sm J \rC \;=\; J_{\lp G_{\doit(I_{W_2})} \rp_{\swig(W_1)}}, \] the only difference between the two expressions being the order of the three pieces in the disjoint union; since $\dcup$ is commutative and associative as a set operation, the two are equal as sets (and as $\dcup$-tuples, since the pairwise disjointness is the same on both sides – by the freshness clause of def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes and the tagged-copy disjointness of $W_1^i$ from $J \cup V$, as already noted in the statement block's carrier paragraph). ✓
  • Output nodes. \[ V_{\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(I_{W_2})}} \;=\; (V \sm W_1) \dcup W_1^o \;=\; V_{\lp G_{\doit(I_{W_2})} \rp_{\swig(W_1)}}. \] (Identical expressions on both sides; no consumption of disjointness, no use of $W_2 \sm J$.) ✓
  • Directed edges. \[ E_{\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(I_{W_2})}} \;=\; \lC v_1^i \tuh v_2^o \st v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E \rC \;\dcup\; \lC I_w \tuh w \st w \in W_2 \sm J \rC \;=\; E_{\lp G_{\doit(I_{W_2})} \rp_{\swig(W_1)}}. \] The first set-builder is identical on both sides (the SWIG-image of $E$); the second set-builder is identical on both sides (the fresh intervention edges, parametrised over $W_2 \sm J$). The two were reached by genuinely different routes – on the LHS the fresh edges arrived via the outer $\doit(I_{W_2})$ acting on $\hat E$ and inheriting them in the bare shape $I_w \tuh w$, on the RHS they entered the inner $G_{\doit(I_{W_2})}$'s edge set $E'$ first and were then SWIG-relabelled $I_w^i \tuh w^o = I_w \tuh w$ via the shorthand identities of Step 1. The disjointness hypothesis $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$ is consumed on both routes (LHS: to identify the head $w$ inside $\hat V$; RHS: to obtain $w^o = w$). ✓
  • Bidirected edges. \[ L_{\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(I_{W_2})}} \;=\; \lC v_1^o \huh v_2^o \st v_1 \huh v_2 \in L \rC \;=\; L_{\lp G_{\doit(I_{W_2})} \rp_{\swig(W_1)}}. \] (Identical expressions on both sides; no consumption of disjointness, no use of $W_2 \sm J$. On the LHS, $L_{\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(I_{W_2})}} = \hat L = \lC v_1^o \huh v_2^o \st v_1 \huh v_2 \in L \rC$ because $\doit(I_{W_2})$ leaves the bidirected-edge component fixed – item iv.\ of def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes; on the RHS, $L_{\lp G_{\doit(I_{W_2})} \rp_{\swig(W_1)}} = \lC v_1^o \huh v_2^o \st v_1 \huh v_2 \in L' \rC = \lC v_1^o \huh v_2^o \st v_1 \huh v_2 \in L \rC$ since $L' = L$.) ✓

Conclusion. By the four componentwise equalities just established and the canonical relabelling identifying the LHS and RHS carriers spelled out in the statement block above, the LHS and RHS coincide field by field on each of $(J, V, E, L)$ and are therefore equal as CDMGs (def def-cdmg). The upgrade from this CDMG-equality to the asserted equality of CADMGs is automatic: both LHS and RHS are CADMGs (their acyclicity is established by the well-typedness paragraph in the statement block – in particular, the RHS's inner $G_{\doit(I_{W_2})}$ is acyclic by claim_3_13, and both outer operations preserve acyclicity by def def:G_node-splitting_intervention and claim_3_13 respectively), so the CDMG-equality transports the acyclicity propositionally, yielding \[ \lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(I_{W_2})} \;=\; \lp G_{\doit(I_{W_2})} \rp_{\swig(W_1)} \] as an equality of CADMGs. ∎

lean_blocks.explanation: This is a `variable` declaration: Lean auto-binds `Node : Type*` and the instance `[DecidableEq Node]` as implicit arguments to every subsequent declaration in the surrounding `CDMG` namespace, sparing us from rewriting the same parameters on each helper and on the main theorem `addInterventionNodes_comm_swig`. The `Type*` notation makes `Node` universe-polymorphic, so callers can instantiate it at any universe level. The `DecidableEq Node` assumption is load-bearing throughout this row: the downstream constructions `Finset.image` (for the lifted subsets `W₁.image IntExtNode.unsplit` and `W₂.image SplitNode.unsplit`), `Finset.filter` and `Finset.union`/`Finset.sdiff` inside `nodeSplittingHard` and `extendingCDMGsWith`, and the `if`-tests inside `toCopy0`/`toCopy1` all need decidable equality on the element type to compute. Decidability is also required at the iterated-tagged-sum carriers `IntExtNode (SplitNode Node)` and `SplitNode (IntExtNode Node)`, transported there by Lean's instance derivation. Stronger structure on `Node` (`Fintype`, `LinearOrder`) is not needed and is deferred to consumers that use it.

    {G : CDMG Node} {W₁ : Finset Node} (hW₁ : W₁ ⊆ G.V)
    {W₂ : Finset Node} {hW₂ : W₂ ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V} :

lean_blocks.explanation: This private helper discharges one of two subset-of-carrier obligations that the main theorem's RHS branch raises before it can type-check. The RHS reads `(G.extendingCDMGsWith W₂ hW₂).nodeSplittingHard _ (W₁.image IntExtNode.unsplit) ?_`, and the `?_` slot demands a proof that the lifted set `W₁.image IntExtNode.unsplit` sits inside the `V`-component of the intervention-extended graph. By `def_3_13` item ii, `V_{doit(I_{W₂})}` is exactly `G.V.image IntExtNode.unsplit`, so the conclusion reduces to lifting the hypothesis `hW₁ : W₁ ⊆ G.V` through the same constructor — every `v ∈ W₁` gives `IntExtNode.unsplit v ∈ G.V.image IntExtNode.unsplit`. The disjointness `Disjoint W₁ W₂` is not consumed: the intervention-node extension preserves `V` literally (no nodes added on the output side), so the helper depends only on `hW₁`. The signature keeps `G`, `W₁`, `W₂`, `hW₂` implicit and `hW₁` explicit so the call site at the main theorem head reads `image_unsplit_subset_extendingCDMGsWith_V hW₁`. `Finset.mem_image` is Mathlib's pointwise characterisation of image membership and is invoked both forward (to destructure `hx`) and back (to build the conclusion).

private lemma image_unsplit_subset_nodeSplittingHard_carrier
    {G : CDMG Node} {hG : G.IsCADMG}
    {W₁ : Finset Node} (hW₁ : W₁ ⊆ G.V)
    {W₂ : Finset Node} (hW₂ : W₂ ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V) (hDisj : Disjoint W₁ W₂) :

lean_blocks.explanation: This second private helper plugs the carrier-subset hole on the main theorem's LHS branch. The LHS reads `(G.nodeSplittingHard hG W₁ hW₁).extendingCDMGsWith (W₂.image SplitNode.unsplit) ?_`, and the `?_` slot demands that the lifted set `W₂.image SplitNode.unsplit` sits inside `J ∪ V` of the SWIG'd graph. The proof case-splits on each `w ∈ W₂`: by `hW₂` either `w ∈ G.J`, in which case `SplitNode.unsplit w` routes through the `J.image .unsplit` summand of `J_{swig(W₁)}`; or `w ∈ G.V`, in which case the disjointness `hDisj` forces `w ∉ W₁`, putting `w ∈ G.V \ W₁` so `SplitNode.unsplit w` routes through the `(G.V \ W₁).image .unsplit` summand of `V_{swig(W₁)}`. Without `hDisj`, a `w ∈ W₂ ∩ W₁ ∩ G.V` would have to land in `W₁.image SplitNode.copy0`, which has the wrong constructor and would not match the goal — so `hDisj` is load-bearing here. Mathlib's `Finset.disjoint_right` converts `Disjoint W₁ W₂` together with `w ∈ W₂` into `w ∉ W₁`, and `Finset.mem_sdiff` witnesses `v ∈ G.V \ W₁`. The role of `W₁` and `W₂` mirrors the sibling helper in `claim_3_11` with the lifted and split sets exchanged.

def flattenSwigDoit :
    SplitNode (IntExtNode Node) → IntExtNode (SplitNode Node)
  | .unsplit (.unsplit v) => IntExtNode.unsplit (SplitNode.unsplit v)
  | .unsplit (.intCopy w) => IntExtNode.intCopy (SplitNode.unsplit w)
  | .copy0 (.unsplit w) => IntExtNode.unsplit (SplitNode.copy0 w)
  | .copy0 (.intCopy w) => IntExtNode.intCopy (SplitNode.unsplit w)
  | .copy1 (.unsplit w) => IntExtNode.unsplit (SplitNode.copy1 w)
  | .copy1 (.intCopy w) => IntExtNode.intCopy (SplitNode.unsplit w)
lean_blocks.explanation: `flattenSwigDoit` realises the LN's "canonical relabelling identifying" the LHS and RHS carriers of the displayed equality. Both carriers encode the same five-piece node universe $J \cup (V \setminus W_1) \cup W_1^o \cup W_1^i \cup \{I_w \mid w \in W_2 \setminus J\}$ but with the `SplitNode`/`IntExtNode` constructor wrappings in opposite orders. The six pattern-match clauses cover every `SplitNode (IntExtNode Node)` constructor combination: an unsplit-unsplit `v` maps to the same on the LHS side (an original node `v ∈ J ∪ (V \ W_1)`); an unsplit-intCopy `w` maps to `IntExtNode.intCopy (SplitNode.unsplit w)` (the intervention symbol $I_w$ for $w \in W_2 \setminus J$); `copy0`/`copy1` of `unsplit w` give the output/input split copies $w^o$, $w^i$ for $w \in W_1$. The two `copy0 (.intCopy _)` and `copy1 (.intCopy _)` clauses are off-carrier — they cannot arise on the reachable RHS subset because the outer `nodeSplittingHard` ranges over `W₁.image IntExtNode.unsplit`, whose every element has the form `.unsplit _`. Lean requires total pattern matches, so these clauses are filled with the semantically nearest filler `.intCopy (.unsplit w)`; they never participate in the `eqViaNodeMap` componentwise equality. The pattern matches use Lean 4's anonymous constructor sugar (`.unsplit`, `.copy0`, `.copy1`, `.intCopy`), with the target type fixing which inductive each constructor belongs to.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- `flattenSwigDoit`: the canonical relabelling RHS-carrier → LHS-carrier; injective on the reachable subset only.
def flattenSwigDoit :
    -- Source: RHS iterated-tagged-sum (extend-then-split). Target: LHS iterated-tagged-sum (split-then-extend).
    SplitNode (IntExtNode Node) → IntExtNode (SplitNode Node)
  -- Original node `v ∈ J ∪ (V \ W₁)`: passes through both untagged constructors unchanged.
  | .unsplit (.unsplit v) => IntExtNode.unsplit (SplitNode.unsplit v)
  -- Intervention symbol `I_w` for `w ∈ W₂ \ J`: stays an `intCopy`, with `SplitNode.unsplit` applied to the underlying `w`.
  | .unsplit (.intCopy w) => IntExtNode.intCopy (SplitNode.unsplit w)
  -- Output-split copy `w^o` for `w ∈ W₁`: outer `.copy0` reshapes into LHS's `IntExtNode.unsplit (SplitNode.copy0 w)`.
  | .copy0 (.unsplit w) => IntExtNode.unsplit (SplitNode.copy0 w)
  -- Off-carrier filler (`copy0` of `intCopy` is unreachable on the RHS subset); chosen for totality only.
  | .copy0 (.intCopy w) => IntExtNode.intCopy (SplitNode.unsplit w)
  -- Input-split copy `w^i` for `w ∈ W₁`: outer `.copy1` reshapes into LHS's `IntExtNode.unsplit (SplitNode.copy1 w)`.
  | .copy1 (.unsplit w) => IntExtNode.unsplit (SplitNode.copy1 w)
  -- Off-carrier filler (`copy1` of `intCopy` is unreachable on the RHS subset); chosen for totality only.
  | .copy1 (.intCopy w) => IntExtNode.intCopy (SplitNode.unsplit w)

lean_blocks.code:
theorem addInterventionNodes_comm_swig (G : CDMG Node) (hG : G.IsCADMG)
    (W₁ : Finset Node) (hW₁ : W₁ ⊆ G.V)
    (W₂ : Finset Node) (hW₂ : W₂ ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V)
    (hDisj : Disjoint W₁ W₂) :
    eqViaNodeMap
        ((extendingCDMGsWith G W₂ hW₂).nodeSplittingHard
            (extendingCDMGsWith_isCADMG_of_isCADMG hG hW₂)
            (W₁.image IntExtNode.unsplit)
            (image_unsplit_subset_extendingCDMGsWith_V hW₁))
        (extendingCDMGsWith
            (G.nodeSplittingHard hG W₁ hW₁)
            (W₂.image SplitNode.unsplit)
            (image_unsplit_subset_nodeSplittingHard_carrier hW₁ hW₂ hDisj))
        flattenSwigDoit
lean_blocks.explanation: The theorem `addInterventionNodes_comm_swig` formalises the LN's displayed commutation: introducing intervention nodes $I_{W_2}$ commutes with the node-splitting hard intervention $\mathrm{swig}(W_1)$, provided $W_1$ and $W_2$ are disjoint with $W_1 \subseteq V$ and $W_2 \subseteq J \cup V$. Because the two iterated operations produce CDMGs in formally distinct carriers `IntExtNode (SplitNode Node)` and `SplitNode (IntExtNode Node)`, the LN's `=` is not directly a Lean equality; instead the conclusion is `eqViaNodeMap RHS LHS flattenSwigDoit`, asserting that the four `Finset` data fields of the RHS, after `flattenSwigDoit` is applied field-wise, coincide componentwise with the four fields of the LHS. The first operand of `eqViaNodeMap` (`extendingCDMGsWith ∘ nodeSplittingHard` on `G`, applied via the two private helpers) is the LN's RHS "extend first, then split"; the second operand (`nodeSplittingHard ∘ extendingCDMGsWith`) is the LN's LHS "split first, then extend"; the `flattenSwigDoit` arrow therefore points first-operand-carrier $\to$ second-operand-carrier. The two helper terms `extendingCDMGsWith_isCADMG_of_isCADMG hG hW₂` (acyclicity for the outer `nodeSplittingHard`) and the two `image_unsplit_subset_…` lemmas fill the carrier-subset obligations the constructors raise. `Disjoint W₁ W₂` is required for well-typedness, not merely for the equality.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Main theorem: swig and intervention-node extension commute on disjoint operand sets, modulo `flattenSwigDoit`.
theorem addInterventionNodes_comm_swig (G : CDMG Node) (hG : G.IsCADMG)
    -- `W₁ ⊆ G.V` because `nodeSplittingHard` acts on output nodes only (`def_3_12`).
    (W₁ : Finset Node) (hW₁ : W₁ ⊆ G.V)
    -- `W₂ ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` because `extendingCDMGsWith` admits any carrier-subset (`def_3_13`'s `I_j := j` convention).
    (W₂ : Finset Node) (hW₂ : W₂ ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V)
    -- `Disjoint W₁ W₂` is the Mathlib `Finset` form of `W₁ ∩ W₂ = ∅`; load-bearing for LHS well-typedness.
    (hDisj : Disjoint W₁ W₂) :
    -- `eqViaNodeMap` (`claim_3_7`): componentwise field-equality after applying the third argument field-wise.
    eqViaNodeMap
        -- First operand = LN-RHS: extend first, then split. Lives in `CDMG (SplitNode (IntExtNode Node))`.
        ((extendingCDMGsWith G W₂ hW₂).nodeSplittingHard
            -- Acyclicity of the inner extension supplied by the private `extendingCDMGsWith_isCADMG_of_isCADMG` wrapper.
            (extendingCDMGsWith_isCADMG_of_isCADMG hG hW₂)
            -- `W₁` lifted through `IntExtNode.unsplit` becomes the SWIG operand on the extended carrier.
            (W₁.image IntExtNode.unsplit)
            -- Carrier-subset obligation discharged by the first private helper above.
            (image_unsplit_subset_extendingCDMGsWith_V hW₁))
        -- Second operand = LN-LHS: split first, then extend. Lives in `CDMG (IntExtNode (SplitNode Node))`.
        (extendingCDMGsWith
            (G.nodeSplittingHard hG W₁ hW₁)
            -- `W₂` lifted through `SplitNode.unsplit` becomes the intervention operand on the SWIG carrier.
            (W₂.image SplitNode.unsplit)
            -- Carrier-subset obligation discharged by the second private helper above; consumes `hDisj`.
            (image_unsplit_subset_nodeSplittingHard_carrier hW₁ hW₂ hDisj))
        -- Third argument: the canonical relabelling that bridges the two iterated-tagged-sum carriers.
        flattenSwigDoit

design_choices:
**A single binary `eqViaNodeMap`, not a literal `=` and not a conjunction of two halves through a shared joint.** The LN displays one equation `(G_{swig(W_1)})_{doit(I_{W_2})} = (G_{doit(I_{W_2})})_{swig(W_1)}`. In Lean the two sides live in formally distinct iterated-tagged-sum carriers `IntExtNode (SplitNode Node)` (LHS) and `SplitNode (IntExtNode Node)` (RHS), which are not Lean-equal as types, so a bare `=` between the two CDMGs is not type-correct. The LN's `=` is implicitly modulo the canonical relabelling identifying the two carriers; that relabelling is rendered explicitly via `flattenSwigDoit`, and `eqViaNodeMap` (from `claim_3_7`) then asserts componentwise equality of the four `Finset` data fields after the relabelling is applied field-wise. Unlike `claim_3_14`(a)'s triple coincidence `iter12 = iter21 = joint`, this row has no third joint form available — SWIG and intervention-node extension are heterogeneous operations, with no `G_{swig+doit(W_1 ∪ W_2)}` collapse — so one `eqViaNodeMap` captures the LN's equality directly.

**A bridge function, not an `Equiv` of types, and only injective on the reachable RHS carrier.** A type-level bijection `SplitNode (IntExtNode Node) ≃ IntExtNode (SplitNode Node)` does not exist: for non-empty `Node`, the source has six reachable constructor combinations per node and the target only four. `flattenSwigDoit` is therefore a total function whose restriction to the reachable RHS carrier `{.unsplit (.unsplit _)} ∪ {.unsplit (.intCopy _)} ∪ {.copy0 (.unsplit _)} ∪ {.copy1 (.unsplit _)}` is the well-defined relabelling. The disjointness hypothesis `Disjoint W₁ W₂` is precisely what keeps that restricted injection well-behaved — without it, an element of $W_1 \cap W_2$ would receive two competing intervention-vs-split-tag readings that collide. The off-carrier cases `.copy0 (.intCopy _)` and `.copy1 (.intCopy _)` are filled with semantically-aligned `.intCopy (.unsplit _)` values purely for totality; they never participate in the `eqViaNodeMap` claim.

**Direction `RHS → LHS`, with the first operand of `eqViaNodeMap` being the RHS and the second the LHS.** Both directions of the relabelling are mathematically valid. The choice `SplitNode (IntExtNode Node) → IntExtNode (SplitNode Node)` matches the `eqViaNodeMap iter joint flatten` convention established by `claim_3_7` (`twoDisjointNodeSplittingsCommute`) and inherited through `claim_3_14`(a): the side whose carrier is being relabelled sits as the first operand, and the side whose carrier is the chosen target sits as the second. The LHS `IntExtNode (SplitNode Node)` reads naturally as the target because the LN writes `LHS = RHS` with LHS on the left and the "first split, then add intervention nodes on the SWIG carrier" reading is closer to the LN prose.

**Hypotheses in the order `(G) (hG) (W₁) (hW₁) (W₂) (hW₂) (hDisj)`.** `hG : G.IsCADMG` is a separate `Prop`-level hypothesis adjacent to `G` rather than baked into `G`'s type — the underlying `structure CDMG` (`def_3_1`) is more permissive than the LN's "CADMG" (it allows cycles), so the LN's "Let `G` be a CADMG" lands as the propositional predicate `IsCADMG := IsAcyclic` carried alongside. This matches `nodeSplittingHard`'s `(G : CDMG Node) (hG : G.IsCADMG) …` signature and the `claim_3_11` binder convention. `W₁` precedes `W₂` to match the LN wording ("Let $W_1 \subseteq V$ and $W_2 \subseteq J \cup V$"), each `Wᵢ` is immediately followed by its `hWᵢ` so call sites read left-to-right like the LN, and `hDisj` comes last because it constrains the pair `(W₁, W₂)` rather than either set individually.

**Asymmetric subset typings: `hW₁ : W₁ ⊆ G.V` versus `hW₂ : W₂ ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V`.** The asymmetry is dictated by the preconditions of the two constructors: `nodeSplittingHard` acts on output nodes and requires `W ⊆ G.V` (`def_3_12`), while `extendingCDMGsWith` admits any `W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` (`def_3_13`'s `I_j := j` convention makes the `J ∩ W` overlap harmless).

**Disjointness on the signature, not as a proof-body side condition.** `Disjoint W₁ W₂` (the canonical Mathlib `Finset` form of $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$) is required *for well-typedness* of the LHS's outer `extendingCDMGsWith`: an element of $W_1 \cap W_2 \cap V$ would have its split copies collide with the unsplit-`W₂` piece in the carrier construction. The helper `image_unsplit_subset_nodeSplittingHard_carrier` consumes `hDisj` on its `V`-branch case-split to route `w ∈ W₂ ∩ G.V` into `(G.V \ W₁).image .unsplit` rather than the wrong `W₁.image .copy0` summand. So `hDisj` lives on the theorem signature, not as an unused premise.

**No case-split on the `W₂ ∩ J` corner cases.** A reader expecting Lean-level case-splits for "what if $W_2 \subseteq J$?" or "what if $W_2 \cap J \neq \emptyset$ and $W_2 \cap V \neq \emptyset$?" will not find them. The carrier-level encoding of `extendingCDMGsWith` makes `def_3_13`'s `I_j := j` convention a type-level fact rather than a side-condition: `IntExtNode.intCopy` only ranges over `W₂ \ G.J`, with the `G.J ∩ W₂` branch absorbed by `IntExtNode.unsplit`. Both `extendingCDMGsWith` and the iterated `nodeSplittingHard ∘ extendingCDMGsWith` automatically degenerate on the `W₂ ∩ J` branch, and `eqViaNodeMap` together with `flattenSwigDoit` tracks the degeneration through both sides. The corner cases live one level below, in the constructor definitions of `IntExtNode` and `extendingCDMGsWith`.

**L-channel encoding via `Sym2 Node` shows up only in the L sub-goal, not in the theorem's shape.** The four binder pairs, the `eqViaNodeMap` componentwise reading, and the `flattenSwigDoit` relabelling are all encoding-agnostic: `eqViaNodeMap` is defined componentwise over `(J, V, E, L)` in the same way whatever Finset type each component carries. The `Sym2`-quotient encoding of `L` from `def_3_1` surfaces only inside the L sub-goal of the proof, where the two-stage lift composes via Mathlib's `Sym2.map_map` and reduces pointwise via `Sym2.map_congr` to the same `Node`-level identity `h_flat_toCopy0_unsplit` that the J / V / E sub-goals call through the `Finset.image_image` + `congr_arg₂` route. No `Sym2`-level lemmas are rolled by hand.

**A private plumbing `IsCADMG` wrapper rather than inlining `extAcyclic` at the call site.** The `extendingCDMGsWith_isCADMG_of_isCADMG` helper is a thin specialisation of `claim_3_13`'s `extAcyclic`, exploiting the definitional `IsCADMG := IsAcyclic` so no separate acyclicity argument is recomputed. Inlining `extAcyclic hG hW₂` directly under the main theorem head was rejected for readability — the named helper makes the `(G.extendingCDMGsWith W₂ hW₂).nodeSplittingHard ⟨isCADMG⟩ …` structure read more transparently. It carries no helper marker because it is not LN content and is referenced only at a single theorem head; the website renderer therefore does not pull it out as a first-class declaration.

**Two standalone subset-of-carrier helpers, each marker-wrapped.** `image_unsplit_subset_extendingCDMGsWith_V` and `image_unsplit_subset_nodeSplittingHard_carrier` are wrapped with three-dash markers because the main theorem head does not type-check without their named conclusions filling the `?_` slots of the outer `nodeSplittingHard` and outer `extendingCDMGsWith` respectively. Implicit/explicit binder splits (implicit `G`, `W₁`, `W₂`; explicit `hW₁` on the first; implicit `hG`, explicit `hW₁`, `hW₂`, `hDisj` on the second) are chosen so the call sites read identically to the helpers in `claim_3_11` and `claim_3_14`.
